questions.promptTex: \text{The Koch snowflake starts as an equilateral triangle. At each step, } \\ \text{ each segment is replaced by ? new segments.}
hints: 1 segment → middle third becomes a bump = 2 new + 2 original outer parts.
questions.explanation: Each segment splits into 4 new (shorter) segments at every iteration.

questions.promptTex: \text{If the starting triangle has perimeter } 3, \text{ what's the perimeter after 3 iterations?}

questions.prompt: As iterations → ∞, the Koch snowflake's perimeter:
questions.explanation: Perimeter multiplies by $4/3$ every step. Infinite limit.

questions.prompt: What about the snowflake's AREA as iterations → ∞?
choices: Approaches a finite value (specifically 8/5 of the original triangle's area)
hints: Bumps add area; the added amounts form a geometric series with ratio < 1.
questions.explanation: The added areas at each iteration form a convergent geometric series. Total area is finite — specifically $8/5$ of the original triangle's area.

questions.promptTex: \text{Fractal dimension of the Koch curve: } \log 4 / \log 3. \\ \text{Round to 3 decimals.}

choices: The fact that coastline length depends on the measuring ruler — finer rulers give longer lengths, with no upper limit.
hints: Smaller ruler → captures more bays → longer measurement.
questions.explanation: Real coastlines have detail at all scales — finer measuring sticks reveal more zigzags and yield longer total lengths. This is a real-world analog of the Koch snowflake's infinite perimeter.

questions.prompt: Why was the Koch snowflake considered a 'monster' by early-20th-century mathematicians?
choices: It is a continuous curve with no tangent line anywhere — defies the smooth-curve calculus that was the math of the day.
It violated the laws of physics.
hints: Calculus relies on smooth, differentiable curves.
The Koch curve has corners everywhere — no tangent line.
questions.explanation: Classical analysis presumed curves were smooth (had tangent lines). The Koch curve is continuous but has corners everywhere — defying that assumption.

questions.prompt: If you reverse the Koch process — pushing bumps INWARD instead of outward — you get:
choices: The 'anti-snowflake', which loses area each iteration
hints: Bumps push inward → bumps subtract area instead of adding.
questions.explanation: Pushing inward gives the 'anti-snowflake'. Each step removes triangles instead of adding them. Still has fractal structure, just inverted.
